text.heading: Roger Federer: A Legend of his Time or of All Time?
text.p: Roger Federer on Sunday claimed the final addition to his glittering shelf of Grand Slam titles with a straight sets win over Robin Soderling of Sweden who had spared teh Swiss master the trouble of perhaps a fourth runners-up place behind Spaniard Rafael Nadal.

But the bigger picture is the fact that the win on the clay courts of Roland Garros equalled for Federer teh record for most Grand Slam wins in tennis history at 14 titles at par with American legend Pete Sampras.
That has brought up arguments of Federer being the greatest ever player in tennis in almost all quarters; almost all as there are sections that still believe Federer doesn't quite cut it.

No doubt he is a fine player having also won five titles on the hard courts of Flushing Meadows in New York and the grass courts in the land of Elizabeth II apiece along with three down under in Melbourne.

His appearance at Sunday's final in Paris; making Federer a well travelled man in his late 20s, was his 15th consecutive appearance in a Grand Slam final and 19th overall, something even his nemesis Rafael Nadal is a long way from achieving if ever it will be and something even the likes of Sampras, Andre Agassi, John McEnroe, Patrick Rafter and Bjorn Borg among many others did not achieve in their playing days.

And that's where the argument can gain credence because the fact that the likes of Sampras and Agassi, Borg and McEnroe could not achieve this only points to the competitiveness of the tour during their time.

A competitveness that makes Sampras' hold on the world number one position in his prime all the more appreciable seeing he had the likes of never-say-die Agassi, sleek Rafter, hard hitting Marat Safin, clay court specialist (yea it's not just Nadal) Gustavo Kuerten, a very much more devastating Lleyton Hewitt and Andy Roddick who then was the kid on the block, Juan Carlos Ferrero of Spain who also had a love for the dirt court among others.

In Comparison, Federer's reign at first was so dominating it was feared no one would ever be able to put a clog as he wheeled away tournament after tournament so thank God for Rafael Nadal whose rivalry ultimately saw him usurp Federer.

And true we can say there have been the likes of Novak Djokovic and Andy Murray but take a look at the fellas Sampras had to deal with and you'll better appreciate Sampras' status as the greatest of all time by some people.

But no matter how many tournaments on the tour Sampras won along with the 14 he Slammed, fact of the matter is that the American never won the Roland Garros, a fact worsened by his infamous inability to somehow never make the latter stages of the French Open.

This is where the argument tilts to Federer's court. He's reached the final of the last four French Open finals; even Nadal can't boast of that, and he has continual made the latter stages of clay courts in the Masters Series tournaments of Madrid, Rome and all.

In the end, it would be safe to say Federer is the greatest all-round player of his time likewise Sampras just as Nadal is unparalled on the clay courts and on and on. Last word, Federer and Sampras are and were grass court specialist and few years ago, Federer did beat Sampras silly in the fourth round of Wimbledon...
